one_line_takeaway: DAMP is a gradient-free, closed-form weight-projection method for removing class-specific directions from pretrained image classifiers, closely matching retraining-quality forgetting on standard benchmarks while its own diagnostics indicate it removes forget-class evidence from deep representations rather than merely suppressing the classifier head as many prior unlearning baselines do.
key_finding: On CNN-5/CIFAR-10 single-class forgetting (Table 1), DAMP achieves Racc=90.33/Facc=0.00 versus Retrained's Racc=90.70/Facc=0.00, closely tracking the retraining gold standard, while baselines diverge in different ways: GAU degrades retain accuracy heavily (e.g., ResNet-18/CIFAR-100: Racc drops to 3.70 vs Retrained's 59.05), KDU leaves substantial residual forget accuracy (e.g., CNN-5/CIFAR-10: Facc=35.50 vs Retrained's 0.00), and SSD/SalUn reach near-zero forget accuracy but with unstable retain-accuracy degradation on harder datasets (e.g., SSD on CNN-5/CIFAR-100: Racc=66.18 vs Baseline's 67.53 and Retrained's 67.86, but SSD on ResNet-18/CIFAR-100 drops to Racc=31.90 vs Retrained's 59.05). The paper's selectivity metric (Fig. 2, CNN-5/CIFAR-10) shows most baselines near zero or negative (GAU -0.29, KDU -0.17, SSD -0.09, Baseline 0.00, LM 0.00, RandRelabel 0.02, SalUn 0.05, DD-FT 1.33) while DAMP reaches 2.19, closer to Retrained's 4.08. Under FGSM/PGD adversarial evaluation (Table S1, aggregated across settings), DAMP retains Retain=81.93/65.08 and Forget=0.0/0.0 under FGSM/PGD respectively, comparable to or better than most baselines, while GAU collapses retain accuracy (25.07/20.02) and KDU retains substantial forget accuracy (32.4/14.6). The bias-shift analysis (Fig. 4) shows several baselines drive forget-class classifier bias strongly negative (used as evidence of head-only suppression) whereas DAMP shows near-zero bias shift, which the authors interpret as evidence that DAMP achieves forgetting via representational removal rather than output suppression.
core_contribution: The paper introduces DAMP (Depth-Aware Modulation by Projection), a one-shot, closed-form, gradient-free weight-surgery method for class unlearning in image classifiers. For each network stage, DAMP computes class prototypes (mean feature vectors) in the input space of the next learnable operator, derives 'forget directions' as residuals of forget-class prototypes relative to the span of retain-class prototypes, orthonormalizes these residuals across forget classes (for multi-class forgetting) via QR decomposition, and right-projects the next operator's weight matrix to reduce sensitivity to that subspace (W' = W(I - alpha_l * Q Q^T)). The projection strength alpha_l at each layer is a parameter-free product of (i) a probe-separability term derived from a logistic-regression probe's held-out accuracy on forget-vs-retain classification at that layer, and (ii) a deterministic depth ramp (l/L) that applies smaller edits in early layers and larger edits in deep layers. The authors also introduce a 'selectivity' diagnostic metric (based on AUC of forget-vs-retain linear separability minus retain-accuracy degradation) and use representational dissimilarity matrices (RDMs), t-SNE, and final-layer bias-shift analysis to argue that many existing class-unlearning baselines achieve apparent forgetting via classifier-head bias suppression rather than true removal of forget-class evidence from internal representations, and that DAMP instead removes that evidence directly.
threat_model: None. This is not a security paper and has no adversary, attacker capability, or attack surface. Machine unlearning here is framed purely as a compliance/utility problem (removing a class's influence from a deployed classifier without full retraining), not as a defense against an attacker trying to extract or misuse retained knowledge. The paper's own adversarial-robustness experiment (Sec. A.4, FGSM/PGD) evaluates whether retain/forget accuracy is stable under input perturbation attacks on the classifier — a robustness-of-utility check, not a threat model for the unlearning mechanism itself (e.g., no membership-inference or model-inversion attack is used to test whether forgotten information is truly unrecoverable).
limitations: The authors explicitly state (Sec. 6) that DAMP approximates a class's representation by its mean feature vector (prototype) and assumes retain/forget class representations are largely linearly separable; this may not hold for highly multimodal or anisotropic feature distributions, and the authors suggest extending to covariance-sensitive subspace estimation as future work. Additional limitations evident from the text but not explicitly flagged as such: the depth-aware coefficient and stage abstraction (L=5) were hand-fit per architecture (CNN-5 blocks, ResNet-18 stem+groups, ViT block groups), and for one setting (Tiny ImageNet + ViT) the authors manually added a constant offset (+3.0) to all alpha values to strengthen the edit, indicating the parameter-free claim required post hoc manual tuning to work well in at least one architecture-dataset combination. DAMP's forget accuracy is 0.00 across nearly all Table 1 cells, matching most working baselines, so the paper's differentiation from strong baselines (DD-FT, LM, RandRelabel) rests mainly on the selectivity/RDM/bias-shift diagnostics rather than on raw Racc/Facc gaps, and those diagnostic metrics (selectivity, RDM deltas) are introduced by this paper itself rather than being externally validated measures.

quantitative_results: CNN-5/CIFAR-10 single-class: DAMP Racc=90.33 Facc=0.00 vs Retrained Racc=90.70 Facc=0.00 vs Baseline Racc=89.60 Facc=82.50 (Table 1)
ResNet-18/CIFAR-100 single-class: DAMP Racc=59.34 Facc=0.00 vs Retrained Racc=59.05 Facc=0.00 vs GAU Racc=3.70 Facc=0.00 (Table 1)
CNN-5/CIFAR-10 selectivity (pp): Retrained 4.08, DAMP 2.19, DD-FT 1.33, SalUn 0.05, RandRelabel 0.02, Baseline/LM 0.00, SSD -0.09, KDU -0.17, GAU -0.29 (Fig. 2)
FGSM/PGD aggregate: DAMP Retain=81.9333/65.0778 Forget=0.0/0.0 vs Retrained Retain=80.1778/66.2111 Forget=0.0/0.0 vs GAU Retain=25.0667/20.0222 (Table S1)
ViT/CIFAR-10 single-class: DAMP Racc=76.47 Facc=0.00 vs Retrained Racc=76.88 Facc=0.00 vs KDU Racc=74.32 Facc=56.30 (Table 1)
